articles.title: The AI Industry is Going Through a Massive Correction
articles.content:
创作清晰、独立且真正有用的内容需要时间。如果您觉得这篇通讯有价值，请考虑成为付费订阅用户。这帮助我更深入地研究、触达更多人、远离广告和隐藏议程，并支持我对巧克力牛奶的严重成瘾。我们采用“量力付费”模式——如果您认同这一使命，很可能有一个适合您的方案。每一次订阅都帮助我保持独立性、避免标题党、专注于深度而非噪音，我深切感激每一位选择支持我们这个小众社群的人。另外——支持这项工作不一定需要自掏腰包。如果您是在职业发展过程中阅读这篇文章，您可以使用这封邮件模板请求报销订阅费用。每个月，巧克力牛奶邪教都会触达超过一百万名建设者、投资者、政策制定者、领导者等。如果您想认识社区的其他成员，请填写此联系表（我永远不会出售您的数据，也未经您明确许可不会做任何介绍）——https://forms.gle/Pi1pGLuS1FmzXoLr6

AI市场正在经历一场调整。不是崩盘。我们正在修正对这一市场运作方式的理解。过去三年里，基准测试取代了实际能力，运营率取代了持久收入，订单积压取代了未来现金流，token价格取代了成本。六月暴露了这些捷径变得多么不可靠。同一个问题无处不在：如何验证您购买的东西？华盛顿无法独立测试导致其召回Anthropic模型的风险。投资者仍然无法比较OpenAI和Anthropic的经济状况。信贷市场可以看到Oracle的资本支出和债务，但看不到其6380亿美元订单积压中埋藏的利润率。首席财务官们可以看到token账单，但看不到代理是否完成了有用的工作。开权模型尽管性能较差，却获得了交易量，因为买家可以测试、托管，并保留他们部署的确切模型。所有这些都讲述了一个市场正在艰难地弄清楚哪些声明可以信任，并对那些不能信任的进行折价。在本文中，我们将讨论：

前沿模型发布变成了需要许可的。商务部使用一项为受控技术转让设计的出口管制理论，禁用了Anthropic的最新模型。证据存在争议，流程绕过了正常监管渠道，法律理论从未在法庭上经过检验。现在每家主要的美国实验室都有了在发布前进行谈判的动机。前沿实验室转向公开市场。Anthropic和OpenAI秘密提交了IPO申请，这意味着经过审计的利润率、亏损、客户集中度和算力承诺最终将取代私人估值和选择性泄露。SpaceX展示了巨大的私人估值遇到公开价格发现时会发生什么。信贷市场开始为基础设施建设中的杠杆定价。Oracle报告了创纪录的增长和6380亿美元的订单积压，但也报告了负自由现金流和另一项巨大的融资需求。需求是真实的。问题是，建设基础设施的公司能否撑到需求付款的那一天。首席财务官们开始对代理进行计量。GitHub将更多使用成本转嫁给客户。Salesforce提出只对完成的成果收费。企业设定了上限，整合了供应商，并开始询问一个代理实际上为所消耗的资金生产了什么。开权模型占据了交易量。中国模型越来越多地处理AI使用的廉价重复层，而专有的美国模型保留了要求最高的工作负载。低价有帮助，但能够检查、托管、复制和保留模型而不依赖美国供应商或政府也有帮助。六月没有解决任何这些问题。它表明没有人愿意再让这些问题悬而未决。这是我们不能忽视的。

**前沿发布在24天内移到了检查点之后**

6月2日，第14409号行政命令创建了一条自愿的“覆盖前沿模型”流程，在发布前给予政府最多30天的访问权限，同时明确否认这是许可制度。Anthropic于6月9日发布了Fable 5和Mythos 5。商务部的指令在三天后发出，Anthropic在约90分钟内全球禁用这两个模型，因为它无法按国籍筛查每个API用户。完整的全球封锁持续了14天，直到6月26日有限的Mythos访问恢复；该指令保持了18天有效，直到6月30日。OpenAI在同一日期部分解除封锁，向大约20个受信任的合作伙伴发布了GPT-5.6。教训很明显：在发布前请求许可现在比冒着事后召回的风险更安全。这也使得模型评估、独立审计、严重性标准和最终的溯源要求变得不可避免，因为政府无法使用无法独立核实的报告来运行许可制度。

**前沿实验室进入了披露倒计时，而SpaceX展示了私人估值遇到公开交易时会发生什么**

Anthropic于6月1日秘密提交文件，OpenAI于6月8日跟进。文件不公开，所以投资者仍然无法比较经过审计的推理利润率、客户集中度、算力承诺、收入质量或亏损。这正是这些文件重要的原因。当前的争论依赖于泄露和私人轮次估值；IPO流程最终将迫使两家公司采用共同的会计框架。SpaceX定价135美元，开盘150美元，在三个交易日内达到约225美元，并在三周内跌破开盘价。该公司进入公开市场时只有4.2%的股份在交易，还有更大的锁定期释放即将到来。纳斯达克修订后的方法论也允许符合条件的IPO在仅15个交易日后纳入纳斯达克100，这意味着被动资金可以在市场有充分时间检验估值之前到达。Anthropic的第一份经过审计的推理毛利率将不仅仅为Anthropic定价。它将重置整个行业的估值方式。

**信贷市场为AI需求与满足需求所需现金之间的差距定价**

Oracle报告了6380亿美元的剩余履约义务、557亿美元的年度资本支出，以及负237亿美元的自由现金流。其五年期CDS利差已在3月达到约198个基点的创纪录水平，接近一年前水平的五倍；六月的结果解释了原因。Oracle必须在大部分收入到来之前为基础设施建设提供资金和建设，这使其面临延迟、利用率、再融资成本、折旧和客户风险。CoreWeave在没有Oracle软件现金流的情况下承担了同样的问题。Micron展示了市场的另一面：当三家供应商控制内存产能而外部人员无法核实其真实分配或生产灵活性时，稀缺性产生了超额利润。国际清算银行随后警告说，债务资助的AI投资和基础设施瓶颈可能以过度投资开始，随后是长期低迷。需求不需要消失。它只需要比融资假设的更晚支付，或以更低的利润率支付。

**代理需求保持强劲，但买家开始设定限制**

GitHub于6月1日将Copilot转为AI积分制，将长时间自主会话、重试、失败搜索和工具调用的成本转嫁给客户。Salesforce采取了相反的立场，只在其Help Agent在无需人工升级的情况下解决问题时才收费，使供应商承担失败的成本。这两种模式都存在，因为当代理可以持续消耗计算资源时，固定费率席位不再有效。企业还开始实施预算限制，并将独立工具整合到平台中，在这些平台上控制采购、数据、安全和遥测。Sonnet 5通过引入一个tokenizer使计费问题变得更加复杂，该tokenizer平均比Sonnet 4.6多产生约30%的token，一项英语测试产生了1.42倍的token。当供应商可以更改相同工作包含的token数量时，每百万token的更低价格意义不大。有用的指标正成为每个已完成任务的成本，包括重试、路由、工具使用、人工干预和失败。

**开权模型占据了交易量，因为买家获得了更低的价格和更多控制权**

OpenRouter发现，到6月初，中国模型已在token交易量上超过美国模型。Vercel的生产数据显示，开源权重模型处理了29%的token，但仅占支出的不到4%，仅DeepSeek就达到了22.6%的交易量。这并不意味着专有前沿模型正在被全面取代。市场正在分化。美国模型保留了最终增量能力证明溢价合理的工作负载，而更便宜的开源权重模型吸收了其下的重复性、高交易量层级。买家还可以测试确切的模型版本、私下托管、控制周围基础设施，并保留访问权限，无论未来的定价或政策如何变化。华盛顿可以限制美国API。它无法远程召回已经在国外私人基础设施上运行的权重。

**我在写这篇通讯上投入了很多工作。为此，我依靠您的支持。如果有更多的人选择成为付费订阅用户，巧克力牛奶邪教可以继续为任何需要的人提供高质量和可及的教育与机会。如果您认为这项使命值得贡献，请考虑高级订阅。您可以以低于Netflix订阅的成本做到这一点（在此量力付费）。想要访问包含我们所有研究的资料库吗？300多个文件，包含我们各种实验的笔记、与前沿团队的讨论，以及对行业下一步发展的见解。获取AI Made Simple的创始成员订阅。想与我交谈/获取我对科技生态系统的见解吗？通过我的任何社交媒体在此联系我，或回复这封邮件。**

Anthropic花了很长时间恳求政府监管开源权重模型，因为它们“太危险了”。结果证明政府购买了他们的恐惧营销，最终却监管了Anthropic。因果报应，不是吗？抛开幸灾乐祸不谈，这件事的发生方式实际上值得研究。主流叙事说Anthropic发布了一个危险的模型，一个越狱证明了风险，华盛顿介入保护公众。主要记录摧毁了这个版本。我们的时间线在这里快速推进：

2026年6月1日：Anthropic提交机密IPO文件。
2026年6月2日：第14409号行政命令创建了一条自愿的“覆盖前沿模型”路径。它给予政府在发布前30天访问权限以评估网络能力，但明确否认任何正式的许可或预先清除权限。
2026年6月9日：Anthropic公开推出Claude Fable 5，同时通过Project Glasswing有限发布Claude Mythos 5。两种模型都在Artificial Analysis指数上以5分的优势超越了GPT-5.5。
2026年6月12日，美国东部时间下午5:21：商务部发布紧急出口管制指令，由部长霍华德·卢特尼克签署，由工业和安全局（BIS）管理。两种模型在90分钟内全球下线。
2026年6月26日：封锁部分解除。Mythos 5访问权限严格开放，仅面向约100家经过审查的美国关键基础设施运营商。
2026年6月30日：商务部正式撤销该指令。
2026年7月1日：Fable 5在全球范围内恢复，附带一个新追加的安全分类器，旨在阻止标记的代码库漏洞利用。

完整的全球封锁持续了14天，直到6月26日有限的Mythos 5访问恢复。该指令保持了18天有效，直到6月30日商务部撤销。两者都没有涉及听证会或法院审查。这里的模型召回是这样的，但发生的事情比人们定价的要重要得多。

这场危机始于一份外部越狱报告，显示了一条提示路径，允许模型映射和暴露大型代码库中的可利用软件漏洞。Anthropic公开称该技术“狭窄且非通用”，指出它属于已存在于其他已部署模型中的漏洞利用类。该漏洞完全绕过了标准漏洞赏金渠道到达。Anthropic的内部漏洞赏金已运行超过1000小时，但没有发现它。

升级路径绕过了标准监管渠道。报告显示，链条从亚马逊CEO安迪·贾西到财政部长斯科特·贝森特，然后直接到商务部。亚马逊是Anthropic最大的投资者、其主导的算力供应商，也是——通过其Nova模型——直接竞争对手。花一秒钟考虑这意味着什么。



召回新模型的先例已经确立——将竞争对手的旗舰模型从全球分发中撤下的证据要求大约相当于一个回拨电话。现在每家实验室都知道竞争对手持有这种筹码，这将很可能引发一场大规模的游说军备竞赛，确保每家实验室都拥有应对措施。法律机制也带来了更大的行业风险。商务部援引了“视为出口”理论。这一原则将外国国民的API会话视为受控技术转让，这一框架最初是为实物蓝图和导弹部件建立的。由于在AWS、GCP和Azure上实现实时、逐用户国籍筛查是不可能的，该限制被适用于所有外国国民，包括Anthropic自己的海外员工。目前模型可执行的最小合规单位是所有人。最后，值得注意的是，当该指令于6月30日解除时，这一法律理论未能经过检验就消亡了。这看似是一场胜利，但实际上这是对行业最不利的结局。由于该理论从未被真正驳倒，它可以被无限次重复使用。各实验室的行为显示了筹码掌握在谁手中。6月26日，就在Anthropic的封锁部分解除的同一天，OpenAI发布了GPT-5.6。他们没有向公众发布，而是将其提供给大约20个政府协调的“可信合作伙伴”，将通用可用性的发布挡在一个审查框架后面，该框架于8月1日最终确定。OpenAI目睹了竞争对手因其事后异议而失去旗舰模型两周，并将召回风险转化为先行请求许可。谷歌延迟发布的Gemini 3.5 Pro在单一来源报道中现被描述为“已获准于7月发布”。“已获准”这个词在两个月前的发布报道中并不存在。在24天内，美国科技生态系统从一个明确声明无许可免责的自愿框架转向了一个事实上的许可制度。没有立法机构对此投票表决，也没有法院对此审查。它完全由行业对Fable宕机那一周的记忆来执行。这一新兴架构有三个部分：

发布前访问：稳定发布的不可协商代价。合作伙伴名单：国家选择最初的20家公司进行分发，将前沿访问转变为配给式稀缺补贴。召回：作为该安排后盾的永久保障，因为行业已经看到它开过一次火而不需要进一步执行。大美国人工智能法案（GAAIA）讨论草案将以强制性第三方审计和每日100万美元的民事罚款换取州优先权的形式将这一结构正式化。对政治戏剧的关注忽略了一个关键问题：许可制度需要验证标准，而目前不存在任何可核查的东西。6月，商务部基于一份无法独立评估的越狱报告召回了有史以来部署的最强模型。该说法来自一个与该模型存在竞争交叉利益的来源，而该模型自身1000小时的安全测试未能复现该问题。国家没有内部评估来确认或反驳风险，没有公认的严重程度量表来分类，没有可咨询的独立审计员，也没有任何超出政治共识的“危险能力”定义。这种干预在认识论上是即兴发挥的。监管者知道一个随意的程序无法在持续诉讼或政府换届中存活。即兴的权力要么制度化，要么蒸发。8月1日的框架表明他们选择了制度化。这一转变强制催生了一个缺失的验证行业。如果国家声称有权在发布前检查模型，它需要独立于供应商声明的评估标准、不为实验室工作的审计员、可重现的严重程度指标，以及对训练输入的监管链追踪。你无法认证你无法追溯的东西。（颇具诗意的是，我们在大约一年前首次预测了验证经济的崛起，这在我们对2026年人工智能的预测中有所详述。市场现在开始验证这一点）

监管序列遵循特定顺序：

能力评估首先出现：许可决策所消费的直接数据层。数据溯源和透明度要求随之而来：当审计发现面临诉讼时所需证据层。这遵循每 major受监管行业的历史路径。证券交易产生了GAAP和会计职业；制药业强制要求GLP和临床注册；航空建立了型号认证。人工智能只是将这一转变压缩到了14天。与此同时，欧盟的通用人工智能（GPAI）执法于8月2日生效，威胁处以全球营业额3%的罚款。两个独立的许可制度正在同时上线。双合规现在是前沿分发的基础条件。关键工程问题是这一整个架构下的测量层已经崩溃。正如第4节所记录的，基准分数经常因回退路由到辅助模型而被扭曲，分词器调整破坏了价格-性能比较，编排层在单模型性能指标背后掩盖了无限制的算力扩展。监管者在评估科学能够可靠提供之前就以法定时间表强制执行严格验证。这个真空将很快被填补。无论哪个实体定义评估参数——无论是标准机构、专业审计公司的整合网络、保护自身地位的现有实验室，还是专业的评估初创公司——都将主导市场。在审计制度中，控制测试的实体控制商业格局。人工智能标准和审计基础设施市场将在四个季度内认真形成。到明年此时，我们将像分析硬件内存分配一样分析评估标准政治。

谈到数字，召回对Anthropic的直接财务成本是有限的——估计在数千万美元，相对于129亿美元每日计算和运营成本的抽象基准。持久的结构性重新定价落在了行业架构上：

多模型故障转移从基础设施成本优化转变为严格的合规要求，结构性削弱了供应商锁定。非美国企业了解到对美国托管前沿模型的访问可以在90分钟内被撤销。Mistral立即利用了这一教训，以主权本地部署为卖点，支撑了其最近的30亿欧元融资。我预计到2026年底，每一项美国前沿发布都将附带直接的政府协调。至少一家主要实验室将在第四季度将其可信合作伙伴监管地位作为企业安全功能来营销（IBM在复苏之路上）。

监管只是修正的一部分。同样的可验证数字需求也波及了资本市场。人工智能部门的金融重心在三十天内从私人轮次转移到了公开注册声明。Anthropic于6月1日秘密提交文件，就在以9650亿美元投后估值完成65亿美元H轮融资四天后。OpenAI于6月8日确认了自己的秘密提交。SpaceX于6月11日定价了有史以来最大规模的IPO，并于6月12日开始交易，以1.77万亿美元估值融资750亿美元，合并的xAI包裹在其中。这一时机并非巧合，也不是崇拜夏至的异教仪式。后期风险投资和主权财富基金已经耗尽了弹药。当你每次融资需要600亿美元以上时，你已经耗尽了私人跨界宇宙。巨大的计算承诺迫使这些实验室走向公开。从这个角度看，IPO浪潮不是胜利的庆祝；而是用你奶奶的养老金作为流动性的绝望再融资，同时为日益焦虑的科技投资者提供退出机会。关于文件的一个有趣事实是，人们假装两家公司文件可以一对一比较，但实际上并非如此。OpenAI和Anthropic的文件都有奇怪的折皱，只是方向不同。OpenAI的问题已披露并量化：泄露的2026年预计亏损140亿美元，对抗以消费者为主的订阅组合。这很丑陋，但至少是一个已入账的指标。Anthropic的问题更安静，更难定价，因为它是一个收入是否能维持的问题。建立在计量代理消费基础上的运营率只有在客户自愿持续消费的情况下才是高质量收入。6月是企业客户开始说不的月份：

微软内部取消了84%至95%采用率的Claude Code。各企业本季度交换了关于代币错误配置的恐怖故事。工程团队发现他们通过配置错误的代理管道烧掉了大量资金的模式现在已经成为企业事后分析的一个独立类型，Kirkland & Ellis等主要律所出现在这些案例中。我们看到了对代币最大化趋势的普遍抵制，这是我们之前报道过的——

Anthropic整个春季的行为表明他们知道这一点。他们在4月中旬到5月中旬的五周内三次提高了Claude Code的容量限制——这一干预最令人信服的解释是试图阻止订阅者流向Codex，后者完成同等任务大约消耗四分之一的代币，已回升至约600万用户。5月14日，Anthropic宣布程序化使用将从6月15日起分割到单独计量的积分。6月15日，他们在生效当天取消了这一变更。你不会在最后时刻撤回计费变更，除非你的客户威胁要哗变。然后，6月22日，发布13天后，Fable 5完全退出平价订阅计划，转向使用积分。一家供应商在其旗舰产品上市两周后就用自有订阅者进行计量，这告诉你关于其单位经济的一切。即使在现在，7月，Anthropic也一直在推迟取消Fable的日期，因为它目前感受到的压力。

所以结论不是哪家实验室在赢。而是没有人目前能够对这些公司进行排名，S-1流程的存在正是为了结束这种混乱。首家打印的审计推理毛利率将重新锚定整个行业的估值。如果Anthropic在10月以或高于其9650亿美元私人估值定价，每一项私人人工智能资产都将以公共基准重新评级。如果跌破H轮价格，下一轮信号将级联传导至共同基金净值、员工RSU以及每一条使用实验室股权作为隐性抵押品的供应商融资链。10月Anthropic的首次亮相是2026年末最具影响力的定日事件。OpenAI倾向于2027年上市看起来不像耐心；更像是让别人先试水。

说到上市，6月发生了一件大事我们需要讨论。SpaceX展示了私人估值最终遇到公开市场时会发生什么。SpaceX的IPO确实引人注目。招股说明书声称2.85万亿美元的总可寻址市场，纽约大学Aswath Damodaran公开嘲笑了这个数字，写道这份文件读起来像是由Grok写的，并指出IPO价格比他自己现金流折现估值高出27%。该结构将一个产生现金的卫星业务与一个深度亏损的人工智能实验室融合在一起。公司2025年净亏损49.4亿美元，紧随其后的是2026年第一季度净亏损42.8亿美元，同时还有77亿美元的季度人工智能资本支出。股价从135美元的IPO价格在三日内涨至约225美元，然后全部回吐。三周内跌破150美元的开盘价，到7月初较上市后高点下跌约25%。其首次债券发行——6月下旬宣布的250亿美元——在纸上看起来是投资级，但在二级市场交易得像垃圾债券。真正的供应甚至还没有到来。IPO时的流通股仅占流通股的4.2%。主要锁定期解除预计在公司首次财报后开始，年底前将有更多批次可用。一些释放取决于股票达到价格目标，所以疲软的股价可能延迟部分供应。但无法消除更广泛的问题。一只交易价格为101倍销售收入的股票正接近大量公开增加的可交易股票。正如我们之前报道的，这种结构是为了给内部人士流动性，而以从未选择该敞口的指数基金持有者为代价。纳斯达克修订后的方法论于5月1日生效，允许符合条件的IPO在15个交易日后进入纳斯达克-100，无需最低流通股门槛。纳斯达克在6月11日单独宣布了五次季度调整，于6月22日生效。



这两起事件虽然独立发生，但共同揭示了 Newly public 的 AI 公司如何迅速被纳入被动投资组合。同样的规则可能让 Anthropic 在 10 月上市后数周内就符合纳入条件。上市帮助 SpaceX 以 600 亿美元收购了 Cursor，几乎没有付出任何代价——这得益于其巨大的估值和增值。我们可能会看到类似的整合继续发生，因为“AI 将吞噬一切，因此投资于分发渠道”这一论题偏向于现有巨头和更大的玩家。S-1 文件最终将揭示这些实验室的真实利润率。信贷市场没有等待。他们已经开始为建设热潮下已经可见的融资风险定价。3 月时，这还只是一个论点。我们认为西方 AI 建设热潮是通过超大规模云服务商的债务、供应商融资、基础设施抵押贷款，以及越来越循环的投资循环来融资的。只要需求按时到来，这个系统就能运转。如果 ROI 时间表落空，加速建设的相同杠杆也会加速重新定价。4 月，问题变得可操作了。电力限制、许可延迟和投机性的吉瓦级公告暴露了已宣布产能与实际可以融资和建设的产能之间的差距。信贷市场在 3 月就已经开始重新定价风险。6 月的业绩报告解释了原因。问题不再是 AI 需求是否真实存在，而是谁来为今天的支出与明天的收入之间的缺口提供资金。

Oracle 在 6 月 10 日发布了第四财季报告。营收达到 192 亿美元，同比增长 21%；Oracle 云基础设施增长 93%；未完成履约义务达到 6380 亿美元，同比增长 363%。按照旧的企业软件评分标准，这是惊人的表现。Oracle 交出了看起来像是该行业历史上最亮眼的财报。但时代变化得很快。所以第二天股价反而下跌了约 12%。为什么？Oracle 在 2026 财年花费了约 557 亿美元的资本支出，却产生了负 237 亿美元的自由现金流。该公司在 2026 财年通过债务融资 430 亿美元、通过股权融资 50 亿美元，然后告诉投资者，预计 2027 财年将通过债务和股权再融资 400 亿美元。

这里需要对纯粹悲观版本的故事做一个重要修正。Oracle 表示，其庞大的 AI 合同中有 750 亿美元涉及客户预先支付 GPU 费用或自行提供 GPU。整个 6380 亿美元的积压订单并非都是未融资负债（这恰好是我父母在我找到工作前对我的称呼；印度父母对儿子写通讯而不去一家体面公司工作的骄傲程度如何，就留给你想象了）。问题在于时间。Oracle 必须在大部分收入到来之前建设基础设施。在此期间，它承担着融资、建设、利用率、折旧和交易对手风险。信贷市场已经注意到了这一点。3 月，Oracle 的五年期信用违约互换利差达到约 198 个基点的历史新高，而一年前约为 40 个基点。市场在为 Oracle 的债务保险时收取了近五倍的费用，这发生在 6 月业绩报告解释原因之前。投资者无法独立核实 6380 亿美元长期合同中嵌入的最终利润率、利用率、取消风险或交易对手持续性。他们能核实的是 557 亿美元的资本支出、负自由现金流，以及另一个巨大的融资需求。所以信贷市场为它能看到的东西定价。（7 月更新：7 月 9 日，标普将 Oracle 从 BBB 下调至 BBB-，仅比垃圾级高一档。标普表示，Oracle 扩张的 AI 基础设施业务正在削弱其传统业务风险状况，并承认低估了建设所需的投资。6 月提出的确认信号之一在一个月内就到来了。）

这里的重点不是 Oracle 即将违约。而是 Oracle 正利用其投资级资产负债表来弥合 AI 公司当前现金流与其未来计算承诺之间的差距。OpenAI 是明显的集中风险。他们在 2026 年第一季度烧掉了 37 亿美元，而收入只有 57 亿美元。路透社无法独立核实这些内部文件，但这种差距足以说明 Oracle 正在融资什么。因此，Oracle 股票正在成为一个不仅赌 AI 需求的杠杆化赌注。它取决于客户能否在融资成本、折旧和基础设施利润率吞噬上行空间之前，将计算承诺转化为足够有利可图的产品。如果我简单地说，所有这些的要点就是：虽然积压订单传统上被视为资产，但需要数百亿融资才能支付的积压订单是……一个问题。CoreWeave 展示了相同的结构，但没有 Oracle 的软件现金流。该公司第一季度末有 994 亿美元的营收积压订单和 20.8 亿美元的季度营收，但录得 5.36 亿美元的净利息支出和 7.4 亿美元的净亏损。该公司还签署了来自 Meta 的 210 亿美元新承诺以及与 Anthropic 的多年协议。同样，需求不是问题。公司必须在几年内确认的合同基础上，今天就为 GPU、电力和数据中心提供资金。如果交付延迟，利用率下降，客户内部化产能，或再融资成本上升，积压订单就会贬值。这不是好事。

6 月 28 日，国际清算银行终于为这种结构命名。其年度报告警告称，AI 投资越来越多地通过债务和复杂融资安排获得支持，而激烈的竞争和基础设施瓶颈增加了过度投资随后出现长期投资衰退的风险。路透社将这一警告总结为高估值和日益复杂的债务融资结构造成的金融脆弱性。循环性通过 SpaceX（上帝保佑 Elon）变得几乎可笑地可见。2 月 SpaceX 收购 xAI 后，Anthropic 获得了 300 兆瓦 Colossus 1 数据中心的全部容量。然后 Google 同意从 2026 年 10 月到 2029 年 6 月每月向 SpaceX 支付 9.2 亿美元，容量包括约 11 万个英伟达 GPU。Anthropic 和 Google 的协议在其所述期限内合计名义价值超过 700 亿美元。但名义价值不是保证收入。Google 可以在 12 月 31 日后以 90 天通知终止合同，如果 SpaceX 未能履行交付义务，Google 可以更早退出。这就是 2026 年的 AI 融资。一家 AI 实验室从竞争对手 AI 实验室的母公司租用计算资源。供应商记录了一份巨额合同。客户记录了对未来计算资源的使用。外部投资者看到名义价值，但无法观察到未来的利用率、合同持久性，或支撑任何一方的经济状况。每个人都得到了积压订单。GPU 仍然需要赚钱。但这不需要我们谈论。毕竟，当我们的仁慈科技寡头通过让我们依赖他们的平台来解决我们社会的所有问题时，谁会在乎利润？在这一点上，还有什么选择？

6 月其他地方，验证到来，价格修正。在内存领域，利润率显示了验证仍然薄弱时会发生什么。Micron 报告第三财季营收为 414.6 亿美元，而一年前为 93 亿美元。其非 GAAP 毛利率达到 84.9%，而一年前为 39%。然后该公司指导下一季度营收约 500 亿美元，毛利率约 86%。这些不是普通的商品周期数字。Micron 的核心数据中心业务报告毛利率为 87%。Micron 还宣布了涵盖 16 个客户的战略协议，约 220 亿美元的承诺。这些协议包括存款、采购义务和定价保护，旨在使当前的短缺对 Micron 来说更持久和可预测。短缺本身是真实的。AI 系统需要大量高带宽内存，而新的晶圆厂和生产线需要数年才能建设和认证。外部人士无法核实的是，稀缺性中有多少是不可避免的。客户和分析师无法独立观察制造商的真正晶圆分配、生产良率、转换时间表，或每家供应商能在多低的利润率下扩张产能。制造商控制着供应和关于该供应的大部分有用信息。当供应集中、产能不透明、客户又急需时，供应商可以在外部人士无法区分物理约束与商业纪律的情况下获取非凡的稀缺租金。Micron 85% 的利润率就是收据。（7 月更新：7 月 10 日，SK 海力士首席执行官表示，2027 年可能带来该行业历史上最严重的内存短缺，需求可能持续超过供应至 2030 年以后。）

后果蔓延到每一个 AI 资本支出预测。基础设施支出中越来越大的一部分现在用于更高的内存价格，而不是成比例地增加可用计算。该行业的第三次重新定价来自宏观数据。6 月 25 日，美国经济分析局将第一季度实际 GDP 增长上调至年化 2.1%。投资、信息服务、专业和技术服务以及耐用消费品制造是主要贡献领域。与此同时，私人国内购买者的实际最终销售仅增长 1.7%。美国经济分析局没有发布官方的“AI 对 GDP 的贡献”。一项私人分解估计，AI 相关支出占第一季度名义增长的约 76%。这个数字很大程度上取决于哪些投资类别被标记为 AI，因此应被视为估计值，而非国民账户事实。这意味着 AI 基础设施在潜在国内需求走弱的同一时刻，已成为美国商业投资的主要引擎之一。

然后在 6 月 17 日，美联储将政策利率维持在 3.50% 至 3.75%。但其预测急剧转向鹰派。中位数预测的 2026 年年底利率升至 3.8%，而 2026 年 PCE 通胀预测中位数升至 3.6%，核心 PCE 升至 3.3%。为什么要谈论这个？简单地说，这创造了另一种循环——AI 资本支出支撑经济增长。更强的增长给美联储更少的降息空间。更高的利率然后提高产生这种增长的基础设施的融资成本。风险最大的公司不是现金充裕的超大规模云服务商。他们是 Oracle、CoreWeave 以及其他被付钱为他们建设产能的杠杆化层级。纳斯达克在 6 月 5 日展示了这种敏感性。在美国经济增加 17.2 万个就业岗位（比共识预期高一倍多）之后，对再次加息的预期飙升，纳斯达克下跌约 4.2%。7 月更新：7 月 14 日发布的 6 月通胀报告削弱了最激进鹰派版本的论点。6 月整体 CPI 下跌 0.4%，同比上涨 3.5%，而核心 CPI 月度持平，同比上涨 2.6%。这降低了立即再次加息的可能性，但没有消除债务融资基础设施对利率的潜在敏感性。

在空头变得过于自大之前，还有一个必要的修正。病毒式传播的说法是，2026 年美国数据中心计划产能的一半已被取消，这是错误的。研究发现，其对真正的超大规模云服务商自建产能的预测在六个月内仅变化了约 1%，而其托管预测变化不到 5%。明显的崩溃来自于将投机性公告计为真实项目。许多项目缺乏融资、租户、设备订单、互联协议或建设。换句话说，它们一开始就不是真正严肃的项目。在信贷警告到来的同一个月，需求仍然巨大。Broadcom 预测第三财季 AI 芯片营收为 160 亿美元，是一年前的三倍多，尽管该预测仍略低于华尔街的预期。OpenAI 还承诺购买最多 750 兆瓦的 Cerebras 容量，据报道扩大的协议价值超过 200 亿美元。这场建设热潮是否能产生足够的收入来证明资本的合理性，取决于客户是否继续为消耗所有这些计算资源的代理付费。6 月给了我们大量可供审视的内容。

我们谈论扁平费率代理定价的崩溃已经有一段时间了。6 月展示了两种替代方案：向客户收取代理消耗的计算费用，或者按结果收费，让供应商承担失败的成本。6 月 1 日，GitHub 用 GitHub AI Credits 取代了 Copilot 的高级请求系统，每个信用额度定价 0.01 美元，根据每个模型 API 费率下消耗的输入、输出和缓存 token 计算。



GitHub的解释很直白：在旧体系下，一次简短的对话请求和一次长达数小时的自主编码会话可能让用户支付相同的费用，由GitHub承担差额，而代理使用的增加已使这种模式“不再可持续”。

基础订阅价格保持不变：Copilot Pro每月10美元，Business版每用户19美元，Enterprise版39美元，但超出配额的用量成为客户的问题，包括搜索失败、重复测试运行、错误的工具调用和重试。一些用户报告称预计账单比平时高出100倍，尽管这些仍是个别报告而非经过审计的全平台数据。GitHub在6月19日将每用户信用消耗添加到其用量指标API中，到7月2日引入了成本中心信用池和独立的支出上限。OpenAI采取了同样的方向，随后又部分逆转。该公司于4月为Business和Enterprise客户推出了按量付费的Codex席位，随后在6月24日停止向ChatGPT Business客户提供新的按量付费席位。现有席位保持活跃，常规Business订阅仍包含基础Codex访问权限，但从实验到计量部署的自助服务路径只存在了不到三个月。最后，Salesforce采取了相反的风险立场。其于6月25日推出的Agentforce Help Agent，原定7月开始按解决次数计费，仅在问题从头到尾自主解决时才收费，当客户提供负面反馈或请求人工服务时不收费。Salesforce在6月未公布每次解决的价格，但结构比数字更重要：在输入定价下，客户在代理尝试时付费；在结果定价下，供应商承担推理成本，仅在能将结果辩护为成功时才获得报酬。Salesforce表示其自身帮助站点已处理430万次咨询，自主解决了其中70%的问题，但结果定价并不能消除验证，只是将其转移。每一次有争议的解决都会变成关于账单的争论：问题是否真正关闭、用户是否返回、人工是否纠正了答案、以及谁定义成功。想象每一次争议都需要提交46页的证明材料，以至于大多数不满意的客户被烦到放弃提交索赔。进步的本质就是如此。两种方法并存的原因是代理打破了SaaS的核心假设——每个座位的消费量以人类注意力为界。Ramp的6月交易数据显示了由此产生的差异：在超过7万家美国企业中，中位数AI支出仅为每位员工每月11.38美元，而前10%的企业支出约611美元，前1%的企业支出高达7449美元。统一费率定价适用于中位数客户，但在最激进的用户身上崩溃（随着更昂贵的模型变得普遍，越来越多的人开始使用代理，成本百分位将大幅变化）。更强的信号来自买家：

据报道，Uber在2026年前四个月就用完了全年Claude Code预算，随后对每种代理编码工具（包括Claude Code和Cursor）实施了每员工每月1500美元的上限。微软提供了更有说服力的案例。其包含Windows、Microsoft 365、Teams、Outlook和Surface的Experiences and Devices部门，开始取消大多数内部Claude Code许可，并在6月30日之前指示数千名员工转向GitHub Copilot CLI。Claude模型仍可通过Copilot访问；微软移除了独立的Claude Code产品，并将使用整合到其自身控制的基础设施、采购和遥测系统中。这一决定说明了为什么企业购买并不像最初设想的那样依赖产出。一款产品可以提高产出，但如果买家拥有可替代品、能够更深入地集成它、且更倾向于将数据、支出和议价能力保留在自己的技术栈中，仍可能失去合同。微软7月发布的一项研究发现，采用命令行编码代理的工程师在四个月内合并的拉取请求增加了约24%，但作者警告合并的拉取请求只是价值的代理指标。微软有内部证据证明可衡量生产力提升，仍然移除了大多数Claude Code许可，因为它不需要租用Anthropic的完整产品来获取这些收益。一旦成本变得可见，企业就会在性能、集成、安全性、采购、可观测性、转换成本和供应商杠杆之间进行优化，而不是简单地购买排行榜上名列前茅的模型。模型可以在基准测试中获胜，而供应商却可能失去预算。支出数据显示了同样的模式。对100家全球2000强企业高管的1月调查显示，在两年内企业LLM平均支出从约450万美元上升到700万美元，受访者预计2026年将再增长65%，81%正在测试或使用至少三个模型系列，65%在可用时仍偏好现有产品。预算在增长，但也在变得更加分散、受管控和可协商。 token价格下跌并未产生无限需求，因为更便宜的智能仍需通过采购流程。一旦买家开始审核账单，计费单位本身就不稳定了。Anthropic于6月30日推出Claude Sonnet 5，入门价格为每百万输入token 2美元、每百万输出token 10美元（截至8月31日），之后升至3美元和15美元。Sonnet 5还引入了一种新的tokenizer，根据Anthropic自身的估计，对于相同输入，与Sonnet 4.6相比产生的token数量增加约30%，具体取决于内容。Simon Willison的独立测试发现，相同文档对英语产生的token数量是原来的1.42倍，西班牙语1.33倍，Python 1.27倍，简体中文几乎没有变化。在英语样本上，促销价格因此相当于每百万旧token等效输入token 2.84美元和输出token 14.20美元，与Sonnet 4.6的3美元和15美元定价大致相当。促销结束后，相同文本的有效成本为4.26美元和21.30美元，尽管价格表看似不变，却是42%的涨幅。Anthropic披露了tokenizer的变化，所以问题不在于隐瞒。问题在于供应商训练客户将token视为通用单位，同时保留重新定义该单位的权力。如果一个tokenizer将相同文档转换为多42%的可计费单位，那么每百万token的美元价格在供应商之间就无法比较。当产品标签不再标识一个固定模型时，比较就更加失效。Anthropic的Fable 5可以将某些风险请求路由到Opus 4.8，而Sakana的Fugu-Ultra通过最多五个步骤的工作流程编排Claude Opus 4.8、GPT-5.5和Gemini 3.1 Pro。Fugu在SWE-Bench Pro上达到73.7分，而Opus 4.8为69.2分，这并非因为Sakana训练了一个单一的更优基础模型，而是因为其路由器可以从多个前沿模型购买工作并组合结果。这是一个合法的结果，但这使得当前的比较框架毫无用处。基准测试假设一个模型回答了问题，价格表假设token具有可比性，排行榜假设测试时计算有界限或已披露。代理系统可以通过路由、重试、隐藏推理、工具使用和可变计算同时违反所有三个假设。因此，唯一有用的比较是每个成功完成任务的成本，按tokenizer标准化，并附上完整的执行记录：调用的模型、重试次数、缓存和非缓存上下文、使用的工具、人工干预次数和失败率。即使这样也很困难，因为供应商控制着大部分证据。一篇关于token计费的5月论文发现，tokenizer模糊允许在模拟攻击中低于检测阈值的情况下多报50.85%，而隐藏推理膨胀在最宽松设置下达到1469%。这意味着，虽然开发者可以用脚本检测Sonnet 5的tokenizer变化，但企业审计层必须跨tokenizer、路由器、隐藏推理、工具调用、缓存规则、折扣、重试和结果定义持续重复这一练习。因此，标准和审计市场是一种尝试，为一个制造商控制着尺子的产品建立一个可信的计量器。信用部门在为基础设施建设定价，首席财务官在为代理定价。然后买家将更多量转向更便宜、更易检查、更难关闭的模型。6月12日，商务部迫使Anthropic禁用Fable 5和Mythos 5，因为它无法实时验证用户的国籍。这两款模型于6月12日在全球消失。Mythos 5于6月26日部分恢复给经审核的美国关键基础设施运营商，6月30日商务部撤销了指令，Fable 5于7月1日在全球恢复。关闭四天后，Z.ai在MIT许可下发布了GLM-5.2，可下载权重，无地区锁定。这一对比解释了大部分开源权重策略。托管模型仍由其开发商、云提供商和所属政府控制。访问可以受限，价格可以更改，用量可以被监控，服务可以在客户不同意的情况下消失。一旦开源权重被下载并部署在私有基础设施上，没有任何供应商可以远程禁用它们或重写访问条款。对于美国以外的买家，特别是政府和受监管公司，这不是对开源的意识形态偏好，而是供应链控制。开源权重也更容易验证。客户可以测试他们将部署的确切模型版本，检查其在自身工作负载上的输出，衡量其真实成本，控制推理环境，并在不依赖供应商不断变化的API的情况下复现结果。这并不能使系统完全透明；训练数据和完整训练过程可能仍然封闭。但这消除了部署时最大的不确定性来源之一：隐藏的模型变化、未披露的路由、变化的tokenizer、地区限制和供应商控制的用量日志。在一个越来越围绕可审计性组织的市场中，持有权重的能力本身就是一种验证优势。然后经济学使决策更容易。OpenRouter发现，到6月初，中国模型已在token量上超过美国模型。Vercel的6月生产数据显示，开源权重模型处理了29%的token量，而仅占支出的不到4%。仅DeepSeek就达到了22.6%的token量，而专有模型继续主导支出和最敏感的工作负载。市场并没有取代前沿；而是在分工。专有美国模型保留了最终能力增量证明溢价合理性的任务，而更便宜的开源权重模型吸收了其下方的重复性、大容量层。我们早在2月就指出中国如何明确投资其模型来处理这个“优质智能、低价格”细分市场（占token量的90%），如下所示——GLM-5.2显示了这一层改进的速度有多快。Z.ai保持了相同的7440亿参数混合专家架构，每个token有400亿活跃参数，但通过强化学习、长上下文训练、蒸馏和推理优化实现了大幅性能提升，而不是再次增加模型规模。这很重要，因为前沿溢价现在衰减得更快：一旦一种能力可以通过后训练复制，开源模型就可以以一小部分价格提供大部分功能，且无需供应商依赖。这就是美国实验室和出口管制政策制定者面临的战略问题。封闭模型更容易监管，但每一条限制都会让关心连续性、主权性和验证性的买家觉得开源权重更有价值。华盛顿可以控制对美国API的访问。它无法召回已经运行在欧洲、印度或海湾地区私有基础设施上的模型。美国前沿在顶端仍然更强大，但它是封闭的、昂贵的、需要许可的。中国的开源权重模型正越来越多地为该前沿以下的一切设定价格。在一个走向验证的市场中，客户可以检查、托管和保留的模型可能比得分略高但可以从别处关闭的模型更有价值。大多数企业决策都是避免责备的练习。有一段时间，基准测试给了买家掩护。你选择了排行榜顶端的模型，如果它失败了，至少你跟从了数据。这个借口越来越难用了。基准测试可以被针对训练，token价格不具有可比性，路由器隐藏了使用了多少计算，最好的模型仍然可能是错误的商业产品。会有其他东西取代它们。这就是为什么验证很重要的原因。



审计、批准评估、每任务成本衡量、可信合作伙伴地位、公开发布的利润率以及部署记录，这些将成为买家用来遮掩决策的新证据。这些衡量标准没有一项是完美的。也不需要完美。它们只需要让决策能够站得住脚即可。这赋予了控制这些证据的人权力。政府决定哪些模型获得批准。云服务商决定哪些模型被捆绑、路由和打折。企业平台控制着使用数据和采购流程。审计机构决定什么算作安全或有效。模型实验室可能构建了智能，但由其他人越来越来决定它是否可以购买、如何衡量其价值，以及在哪里部署。实验室本希望智能成为每个行业底层的平台。那些会将神圣的硅谷效率和创新精神带给世界每个领域的颠覆者。他们可能反而会成为受政府、云服务商和企业软件公司控制的平台内部的供应商。生活真的很有幽默感，不是吗？感谢你的阅读，祝你有美好的一天，Dev <3

The AI market is going through a correction. Not a crash. We are correcting what we thought we understood about how this market works.

For the last three years, benchmarks stood in for capability, run rates for durable revenue, backlogs for future cash flow, and token prices for cost. June showed how unreliable those shortcuts had become.

The same question appeared everywhere: how do you verify what you are buying? Washington could not independently test the risk that led it to recall Anthropic’s models. Investors still cannot compare OpenAI and Anthropic’s economics. Credit markets can see Oracle’s capex and debt, but not the margins buried inside its $638B backlog. CFOs can see token bills, but not whether agents completed useful work. Open weights, despite inferior performance, gained volume because buyers can test, host, and keep the exact model they deploy.

All this tells the story of a market is not deciding struggling to figure out which claims can be trusted, and discounting the ones that cannot. In this article we will talk about:

  • Frontier model releases became permissioned. The Department of Commerce disabled Anthropic’s newest models using an export-control theory designed for controlled technology transfers. The evidence was contested, the process bypassed normal regulatory channels, and the legal theory was never tested in court. Every major US lab now has an incentive to negotiate before launching.

  • The frontier labs moved toward public markets. Anthropic and OpenAI filed confidentially for IPOs, which means audited margins, losses, customer concentration, and compute commitments will eventually replace private marks and selective leaks. SpaceX showed what can happen when an enormous private valuation meets public price discovery.

  • Credit markets started pricing the buildout’s leverage. Oracle reported record growth and a $638B backlog, but also negative free cash flow and another enormous financing requirement. The demand is real. The question is whether the companies building the infrastructure can survive the wait for that demand to pay.

  • CFOs started metering the agents. GitHub moved more usage costs onto customers. Salesforce offered to charge only for completed outcomes. Enterprises imposed caps, consolidated vendors, and started asking what an agent actually produced for the money it consumed.

  • Open-weight models took the volume. Chinese models increasingly handled the cheap, repetitive layer of AI usage while proprietary US models retained the most demanding workloads. Lower prices helped, but so did the ability to inspect, host, reproduce, and retain the model without depending on a US vendor or government.

June did not resolve any of these questions. It showed that nobody is willing to leave them unanswered anymore. And that’s not something we can overlook.

Executive Highlights (TL;DR of the article)

  • Frontier releases moved behind a checkpoint in 24 days. On June 2, Executive Order 14409 created a voluntary covered-frontier-model process with up to 30 days of government access before release, while explicitly denying that this was a licensing regime. Anthropic launched Fable 5 and Mythos 5 on June 9. Commerce issued its directive three days later, and Anthropic disabled both models globally within roughly 90 minutes because it could not screen every API user by nationality. The complete global block lasted 14 days, until limited Mythos access returned on June 26; the directive remained active for 18 days, until June 30. OpenAI launched GPT-5.6 to roughly 20 trusted partners on the same day the block partially lifted. The lesson was obvious: asking permission before launch is now safer than risking a recall afterward. This also makes model evaluations, independent audits, severity standards, and eventually data-provenance requirements unavoidable, because the government cannot run a permission regime using reports it cannot independently check.

  • The frontier labs moved onto a disclosure clock, while SpaceX showed what happens when a private valuation meets public trading. Anthropic filed confidentially on June 1 and OpenAI followed on June 8. The filings are not public, so investors still cannot compare audited inference margins, customer concentration, compute commitments, revenue quality, or losses. That is why the filings matter. The current debate relies on leaks and private-round marks; the IPO process will eventually force both companies into a common accounting framework. SpaceX priced at $135, opened at $150, reached roughly $225 in three sessions, and fell below its opening price within three weeks. The company entered public markets with only 4.2% of its shares trading and much larger lockup releases still ahead. Nasdaq’s revised methodology also allows qualifying IPOs into the Nasdaq-100 after only 15 trading days, which means passive capital can arrive before the market has had much time to test the valuation. Anthropic’s first audited inference gross margin will do more than price Anthropic. It will reset how the entire sector is valued.

  • Credit markets priced the gap between AI demand and the cash required to serve it. Oracle reported $638B in remaining performance obligations, $55.7B in annual capex, and negative $23.7B in free cash flow. Its five-year CDS spread had already reached a record of roughly 198 basis points in March, almost five times its level one year earlier; the June results explained why. Oracle must finance and build the infrastructure before most of the revenue arrives, leaving it exposed to delays, utilization, refinancing costs, depreciation, and customer risk. CoreWeave carries the same problem without Oracle’s software cash flows. Micron showed the other side of the market: when three suppliers control memory capacity and outsiders cannot verify their real allocations or production flexibility, scarcity produces extraordinary margins. The Bank for International Settlements then warned that debt-funded AI investment and infrastructure bottlenecks could end in overinvestment followed by a prolonged bust. Demand does not need to disappear. It only needs to pay later, or at lower margins, than the financing assumes.

  • Agent demand stayed strong, but buyers started setting limits. GitHub moved Copilot to AI Credits on June 1, transferring the costs of long autonomous sessions, retries, failed searches, and tool calls to customers. Salesforce took the opposite position by charging only when its Help Agent resolves an issue without human escalation, leaving the vendor to absorb the cost of failure. Both models exist because flat-rate seats no longer work when agents can consume compute continuously. Enterprises also began imposing budgets and consolidating standalone tools into platforms where they controlled procurement, data, security, and telemetry. Sonnet 5 made the billing problem harder by introducing a tokenizer that produces roughly 30% more tokens on average than Sonnet 4.6, with one English test producing 1.42 times as many. A lower price per million tokens means little when the vendor can change how many tokens the same work contains. The useful metric is becoming cost per completed task, including retries, routing, tool use, human intervention, and failures.

  • Open-weight models took the volume because buyers got lower prices and more control. OpenRouter found that Chinese models had overtaken US models in token volume by early June. Vercel’s production data showed open-weight models handling 29% of tokens while accounting for less than 4% of spending, with DeepSeek alone reaching 22.6% of volume. This does not mean proprietary frontier models are being replaced everywhere. The market is splitting. US models retain the workloads where the final increment of capability justifies the premium, while cheaper open-weight models absorb the repetitive, high-volume layer underneath. Buyers can also test the exact model version, host it privately, control the surrounding infrastructure, and retain access regardless of future pricing or policy changes. Washington can restrict an American API. It cannot remotely recall weights already running on private infrastructure abroad.

Section 1. AI Models Releases are getting very complicated

Anthropic has spent a long time begging the government to regulate open-weight models because they’re “too dangerous”. Turns out the government bought their fearmongering and ended up regulating Anthropic. Karma’s a bitch, ain’t it?

Schedeanfruede aside, the way it happened is actually worth studying.

1.1. What Actually Happened Versus What the Press Reported

The mainstream narrative says Anthropic shipped a dangerous model, a jailbreak proved the risk, and Washington stepped in to protect the public. The primary record destroys this version.

Our timeline here moves fast:

  • June 1, 2026: Anthropic files confidentially for its IPO.

  • June 2, 2026: Executive Order 14409 creates a voluntary “covered frontier model” path. It gives the government 30 days of pre-release access to evaluate cyber capabilities but explicitly disclaims any formal licensing or preclearance authority.

    June 9, 2026: Anthropic launches Claude Fable 5 publicly alongside a limited release of Claude Mythos 5 through Project Glasswing. Both models clear GPT-5.5 by five points on the Artificial Analysis index.

  • June 12, 2026, 5:21 PM ET: The Commerce Department issues an urgent export-control directive signed by Secretary Howard Lutnick and managed by the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS). Both models go dark globally inside 90 minutes.

  • June 26, 2026: The block partially lifts. Mythos 5 access opens strictly for roughly 100 vetted US critical infrastructure operators.

  • June 30, 2026: Commerce officially revokes the directive.

  • July 1, 2026: Fable 5 returns globally with a newly appended safety classifier designed to block the flagged codebase exploit.

The complete global block lasted 14 days, until limited Mythos 5 access returned on June 26. The directive remained active for 18 days, until the Department of Commerce revoked it on June 30. Neither involved a hearing or court review.

The model recall here is so and so, but how it happened is a much bigger deal than people are pricing. The crisis began with an external jailbreak report showing a prompt path that allowed the model to map and surface exploitable software vulnerabilities across large codebases. Anthropic publicly called the technique “narrow and non-universal,” noting it belonged to an exploit class already present in other deployed models. The exploit arrived entirely outside standard bounty channels after Anthropic’s internal bug bounty had run for over 1,000 hours without producing it.

The escalation path bypassed standard regulatory channels. Reports indicate the chain ran from Amazon CEO Andy Jassy to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, then directly to Commerce. Amazon is Anthropic’s largest investor, its dominant compute vendor, and—via its Nova models—a direct competitor.

Take a second to consider what that means. The precedent for recalling new models is set— the evidence required to pull a rival’s frontier model from global distribution is roughly one returned phone call. Every lab now knows its competitors hold this leverage, and this will likely trigger a massive lobbying arms race to ensure that every lab has countermeasures against this.

The legal mechanism also creates a larger industry risk. Commerce invoked the “deemed export” theory. This doctrine treats a foreign national’s API session as a controlled technology transfer, a framework originally built for physical blueprints and missile components. Because real-time, per-user nationality screening across AWS, GCP, and Azure is impossible, the restriction is applied globally to all foreign nationals, including Anthropic’s own overseas employees. The minimum enforceable compliance unit for models is currently everyone.

Finally, it is worth noting that the legal theory died untested when the directive was lifted on June 30. This might seem like a win, but this is actually the worst outcome for the industry. Since the theory was never actually struck down, it is infinitely reusable.

The behavior of the labs shows where the leverage sits.

1.2. Why Every Lab Now Asks Permission First

On June 26, the same day Anthropic’s block partially lifted, OpenAI launched GPT-5.6. They did not release it to the public. They shipped it to roughly 20 government-coordinated “trusted partners,” gating general availability behind a review framework that finalizes on August 1. OpenAI watched a rival lose its flagship model for two weeks over an after-the-fact objection and converted recall risk into permission by asking first. Google’s delayed Gemini 3.5 Pro is now described in single-sourced reporting as “cleared for July.” Cleared is a word that did not exist in launch coverage two months ago.

In 24 days, the US technology ecosystem shifted from a voluntary framework with an explicit no-licensing disclaimer to a de facto licensing regime. No legislature voted on it, and no court reviewed it. It is enforced entirely by the industry’s memory of the week Fable went dark.

This emerging architecture has three parts:

  1. Pre-release access: The non-negotiable price of a stable launch.

  2. Partner lists: The state selects the initial 20 companies for distribution, turning frontier access into an allocated scarcity subsidy.

  3. The recall: A permanent backstop that sits behind the arrangement, requiring no further enforcement because the industry saw it fire once.

The Great American AI Act (GAAIA) discussion draft would formalize this exact structure with mandatory third-party audits and $1 million-a-day civil penalties traded against state preemption.

1.3. Why AI Standards and Audit Infrastructure Are Now Inevitable

The focus on the political drama missed something crucial: a permission regime requires verification standards, and right now nothing checkable exists.

In June, Commerce recalled the most capable model ever deployed based on a jailbreak report it could not independently evaluate. The claim came from a source with competitive cross-interests against a model whose own 1,000-hour safety testing failed to reproduce it. The state had no internal evaluations to confirm or refute the risk, no accepted severity scale to categorize it, no independent auditor to consult, and no definition of “dangerous capability” beyond political consensus. The intervention was epistemically improvised. The regulators know an arbitrary process cannot survive sustained litigation or a changing administration. Improvised power either institutionalizes or evaporates. The August 1 framework signals they have chosen institutionalization.

This shift forces a missing verification industry into existence. If the state claims the right to check models before release, it needs evaluation standards independent of vendor claims, auditors who do not work for the labs, reproducible severity metrics, and chain-of-custody tracking over training inputs. You cannot certify what you cannot trace.

(somewhat poetically, we first predicted the rise of the verifier economy almost exactly an year ago, something we elaborated on in our predictions for AI in 2026. The market is starting to validate this)

The regulatory sequence follows a specific order:

  • Capability evals arrive first: The immediate data layer the permission decision consumes.

  • Data provenance and transparency mandates follow: The necessary evidence layer required when an audit finding faces litigation.

This follows the historical path of every major regulated industry. Securities trading generated GAAP and the accounting profession; pharmaceuticals forced GLP and clinical registries; aviation established type certification. AI simply compressed this transition into 14 days. Concurrently, the European Union’s General Purpose AI (GPAI) enforcement goes live on August 2, threatening fines up to 3% of global turnover. Two independent permission regimes are coming online simultaneously. Dual-compliance is now table stakes for frontier distribution.

The critical engineering problem is that the measurement layer underneath this entire architecture is broken. As Section 4 documents, benchmark scores are regularly distorted by fallback routing to secondary models, tokenizer adjustments break price-performance comparisons, and orchestration layers mask unbounded compute scaling behind single-model performance metrics. Regulators have mandated strict verification on a statutory timeline before evaluation science can reliably deliver it.

This vacuum will be filled quickly. Whichever entities define the evaluation parameters—whether standards bodies, a consolidated network of professional audit firms, incumbent labs protecting their position, or specialized evaluation startups—will dictate the market. In an audit regime, the entity that controls the test controls the commercial landscape. The AI standards-and-audit infrastructure market will form in earnest inside four quarters. By this time next year, we will analyze evaluation-standards politics the way we analyze hardware memory allocation.

Talking about numbers, the direct financial cost of the recall to Anthropic was bounded—estimated in the tens of millions against an abstract baseline of $129 million in daily compute and operational run-rate. The lasting structural repricing landed on the architecture of the industry:

  1. Multi-model failover moved from an infrastructure cost-optimization to a strict compliance requirement, structurally weakening vendor lock-in.

  2. Non-US enterprises learned that access to US-hosted frontier models can be revoked in 90 minutes. Mistral capitalized on this lesson immediately, leveraging a sovereign, on-premise deployment pitch to anchor its recent 3-billion-euro capital raise.

I expect every US frontier launch through the end of 2026 to ship with direct government coordination attached. At least one major lab will market its trusted-partner regulatory status as an enterprise security feature by Q4 (IBM on the comeback boizzz).

Regulation was only one part of the correction. The same demand for verifiable numbers reached the capital markets.

Section 2. What Do the Anthropic and OpenAI S-1s Show and Why Did SpaceX Stock Crash?

The AI sector’s center of financial gravity just moved from private rounds to public registration statements in thirty days. Anthropic filed confidentially on June 1, four days after closing a $65 billion Series H at a $965 billion post-money valuation. OpenAI confirmed its own confidential filing on June 8. SpaceX priced the largest IPO in history on June 11 and started trading on June 12, raising $75 billion at a $1.77 trillion valuation with the merged xAI riding inside the wrapper.

The timing isn’t a coincidence or a pagan ritual to honor the summer solstice. Late-stage venture capitalists and sovereign wealth funds are out of dry powder. When you need $60 billion or more per raise, you have exhausted the private crossover universe. The massive compute commitments have forced these labs public. Seen from that lens, the IPO wave isn’t a victory lap; it’s a desperate refinancing to use your grandma’s pension fund as liquidity, while providing the increasingly anxious tech investors an exit opportunity.

2.1. Why Are Both Lab Valuations Currently Unverifiable?

An interesting fact about the filings is that comparing both companies’ filings isn’t as one ot one as people are pretending. OpenAI and Anthropic both have weird wrinkles in their filings, just in different directions.

OpenAI’s problem is disclosed and quantified: a leaked $14 billion projected loss for 2026 against a consumer-heavy subscriber mix. It is ugly, but at least it is a written-down metric. Anthropic’s problem is quieter and much harder to price because it is a question of whether its revenue holds. A run rate built on metered agentic consumption is high-quality revenue only if customers keep consuming voluntarily. June was the month enterprise customers started saying no:

  • Microsoft canceled Claude Code internally at 84% to 95% adoption.

  • Enterprises spent the quarter swapping horror stories about token misconfiguration.

  • The pattern of an engineering team discovering they burned an absurd sum through a misconfigured agent pipeline is now its own genre of corporate post-mortems, with major law firms like Kirkland & Ellis surfacing in these accounts.

We saw a pretty general push back against the tokenmaxxing trend, something we covered here—

Anthropic’s own behavior through the spring shows they know this. They raised Claude Code capacity limits three times in five weeks between mid-April and mid-May—an intervention most convincingly explained as trying to stop subscriber bleed toward Codex, which consumes roughly four times fewer tokens for equivalent tasks and has climbed back to around 6 million users.

On May 14, Anthropic announced that programmatic usage would split onto separately metered credits starting June 15. On June 15, they canceled the change the day it was due to take effect. You do not pull a billing change at the eleventh hour unless your customers threaten to mutiny. Then, on June 22, thirteen days after launch, Fable 5 exited flat subscription plans entirely to move to usage credits. A vendor metering its own flagship against its own subscribers two weeks into the product’s life is telling you everything you need to know about its unit economics. Even now, in July, Anthropic has constantly pushed back its dates for taking Fable away because of the pressure it’s feeling right now.

So the takeaway isn’t that either lab is winning. It is that nobody can currently rank these companies, and the S-1 process exists precisely to end that confusion. The first audited inference gross margin that prints will re-anchor the valuation of the entire sector. If Anthropic prices at or above its $965 billion private mark in October, every private AI asset re-rates against a public benchmark. If it breaks below the Series H price, the down-round signal will cascade through mutual-fund marks, employee RSUs, and every vendor-financing chain using lab equity as implicit collateral. October’s Anthropic debut is the single most consequential dated event of late 2026. OpenAI leaning toward a 2027 listing doesn’t look like patience; it looks like letting someone else test the ice.

Speaking of going public, there was a massive event in June that we need to talk about. SpaceX showed what happens when a private valuation finally meets a public market.

2.2. How Did SpaceX and Nasdaq Structure the Ultimate Retail Liquidity Dump?

The SpaceX IPO was certainly eventful. The prospectus claimed a $28.5 trillion total addressable market, a figure NYU’s Aswath Damodaran publicly ridiculed, writing that the document read like it was “written by Grok” and pointing out the IPO price sat 27% above his own discounted cash flow value. The structure fused a cash-generating satellite business to a deeply unprofitable AI lab. The company posted a $4.94 billion net loss for 2025, followed by a $4.28 billion net loss in Q1 2026 alone alongside $7.7 billion of quarterly AI capital expenditure.

The stock ran from its $135 IPO price to roughly $225 in three trading days, then gave all of it back. It traded below its $150 opening price within three weeks and fell roughly 25% off its post-IPO high by early July. Its first-ever bond issuance—$25 billion announced in late June—is investment-grade on paper but trading like absolute junk in the secondary market.

And the real supply has not even arrived yet. The float at IPO was only 4.2% of shares outstanding. Major lockup releases are expected to begin after the company’s first earnings report, with further tranches becoming available through the end of the year. Some releases depend on the stock meeting price targets, so a weak share price can delay part of the supply. It cannot remove the broader problem. A stock trading at 101 times sales is approaching a large, published increase in tradable shares.

As we covered before, this structure was built to give insiders exit liquidity at the expense of index-fund holders who never chose the exposure. Nasdaq’s revised methodology took effect on May 1, allowing qualifying IPOs to enter the Nasdaq-100 after 15 trading days without a minimum public-float threshold. Nasdaq separately announced five quarterly additions on June 11, effective June 22. The two events were separate, but together they showed how quickly newly public AI companies could be routed into passive portfolios. The same rules could allow Anthropic to qualify within weeks of an October debur.

The public listing helped SpaceX acquire Cursor for 60 billion USD, while giving away almost nothing thanks to their massive valuation and appreciation. We might see similar consolidations happen as the “AI will eat everything, so invest in distribution” thesis biases towards incumbents and bigger players.

The S-1s will eventually reveal the labs’ real margins. Credit markets did not wait. They started pricing the financing risk already visible underneath the buildout.

Section 3. How Credit Markets Started Pricing the AI Buildout

In March, this was still a thesis. We argued that the Western AI buildout was being financed through hyperscaler debt, vendor financing, infrastructure-backed loans, and increasingly circular investment loops. The system worked as long as demand arrived on schedule. Miss the ROI timeline, and the same leverage that accelerated the buildout would accelerate the repricing.

In April, the problem became operational. Power constraints, permitting delays, and speculative gigawatt announcements exposed the gap between announced capacity and capacity that could actually be funded and built.

Credit markets had started repricing the risk by March. June’s earnings showed why.

The question was no longer whether AI demand was real. It was who would finance the gap between today’s spending and tomorrow’s revenue.

3.1. Oracle’s CDS Spread Was the Audit Opinion Nobody Asked For

Oracle reported fiscal Q4 on June 10. Revenue reached $19.2B, up 21%; Oracle Cloud Infrastructure grew 93%; and remaining performance obligations reached $638B, up 363% year over year. On the old enterprise software scorecard, this was ridiculous. Oracle had produced what looked like the greatest earnings print in the industry’s history. But times are changin’ pretty fast.

So instead, the stock fell roughly 12% the next day.

Why?

Oracle spent approximately $55.7B on capital expenditure in FY26 and generated negative $23.7B of free cash flow. It raised $43B in debt and $5B in equity during FY26, then told investors it expected to raise another $40B through debt and equity in FY27.

Now, there is an important correction to the pure doom version of the story. Oracle said $75B of its large AI contracts involved customers either prepaying for GPUs or supplying the GPUs themselves. The entire $638B backlog is not an unfunded liability (which coincidentally is what my parents called me before I got a job; how proud Indian parents are of their son writing his newsletter instead of working for a respectable company is left to your imagination).

The problem is timing.

Oracle must build the infrastructure before most of the revenue arrives. It carries the financing, construction, utilization, depreciation, and counterparty risks in between. This is something that the credit market had already noticed. In March, Oracle’s five-year credit default swap spread reached a record of roughly 198 basis points, up from around 40 basis points one year earlier. The market was charging nearly five times as much to insure Oracle’s debt before the June results explained why.

Investors cannot independently verify the ultimate margins, utilization, cancellation risk, or counterparty durability embedded in $638B of long-dated contracts. They can verify $55.7B of capex, negative free cash flow, and another enormous financing requirement. So credit priced what it could see.

(July update: On July 9, S&P downgraded Oracle from BBB to BBB-, one notch above junk. S&P said Oracle’s expanding AI infrastructure business was weakening its traditional business-risk profile and admitted that it had underestimated how much investment the buildout would require. One of June’s proposed confirmation signals arrived within a month.)

The point here isn’t that Oracle is about to default. It is that Oracle is using its investment-grade balance sheet to bridge the gap between AI companies’ current cash flows and their future compute commitments.

OpenAI is the obvious concentration risk. They OpenAI burned $3.7B during the first quarter of 2026 against $5.7B of revenue. Reuters could not independently verify the internal documents, but the mismatch is large enough to show what Oracle is financing around. Oracle equity is therefore becoming a leveraged bet on more than AI demand. It depends on customers converting compute commitments into sufficiently profitable products before financing costs, depreciation, and infrastructure margins consume the upside.

If I am to put all of that simply, the 2-line takeaway is that while a backlog is traditonally seen as an asset, a backlog that requires tens of billions in financing before it pays is a …problem.

CoreWeave shows the same structure without Oracle’s software cash flows. It ended Q1 with $99.4B of revenue backlog and $2.08B of quarterly revenue, but recorded $536M of net interest expense and a $740M net loss. The company also signed a new $21B commitment from Meta and a multi-year agreement with Anthropic.

Again, demand is not the problem.

The company must finance GPUs, power, and data centers today against contracts recognized over several years. If delivery slips, utilization falls, customers internalize capacity, or refinancing costs rise, the backlog becomes less valuable. That is not good.

On June 28, the Bank for International Settlements finally named the structure. Its annual report warned that AI investment was increasingly being supported by debt and complex financing arrangements, while intense competition and infrastructure bottlenecks increased the risk of overinvestment followed by a prolonged investment bust. Reuters summarized the warning as a growing financial vulnerability created by high valuations and increasingly complex debt-financing structures.

The circularity became almost comically visible through SpaceX (god bless Elon). After SpaceX acquired xAI in February, Anthropic took the full capacity of the 300MW Colossus 1 data center. Google then agreed to pay SpaceX $920M per month from October 2026 through June 2029 for capacity including roughly 110,000 Nvidia GPUs.

The Anthropic and Google agreements carried a combined headline value of more than $70B over their stated terms. But headline value was not guaranteed revenue. Google could terminate after December 31 with 90 days’ notice and could exit earlier if SpaceX failed to meet its delivery obligations.

And that is AI financing in 2026. An AI lab rents compute from the parent company of a competing AI lab. The supplier records a massive contract. The customer records access to future compute. Outside investors see the headline value but cannot observe future utilization, contract durability, or the economics supporting either side.

Everyone gets a backlog.

The GPUs still have to earn the money. But that isn’t something we need to talk about. After all, who cares about profits when our benevolent tech oligarchs will solve all our society’s problems by making us dependent on their platforms? At that point, what is the alternative?

3.2. The Memory Oligopoly Learned What Unverifiable Capacity Is Worth

Everywhere else in June, verification arrived and prices corrected.

In memory, the margins showed what happens when verification remains weak.

Micron reported fiscal Q3 revenue of $41.46B, up from $9.30B one year earlier. Its non-GAAP gross margin reached 84.9%, compared with 39% one year earlier. The company then guided to $50B of revenue and approximately 86% gross margin for the following quarter. Those are not ordinary commodity-cycle numbers. Micron’s core data-center business reported an 87% gross margin.

Micron also announced strategic agreements covering 16 customers and approximately $22B of commitments. The agreements included deposits, purchase obligations, and pricing protections intended to make the current shortage more durable and predictable for Micron.

The shortage itself is real. AI systems require enormous quantities of high-bandwidth memory, while new fabrication plants and production lines take years to build and qualify.

What outsiders cannot verify is how much of the scarcity is unavoidable. Customers and analysts cannot independently observe manufacturers’ true wafer allocations, production yields, conversion schedules, or how aggressively each supplier could expand capacity at a lower margin. The manufacturers control the supply and most of the useful information about that supply. When supply is concentrated, capacity is opaque, and customers are desperate, suppliers can capture extraordinary scarcity rents without outsiders being able to distinguish physical constraint from commercial discipline.

Micron’s 85% margin is the receipt.

(July update: On July 10, SK Hynix’s chief executive said 2027 could bring the worst memory shortage in the industry’s history and that demand may continue exceeding supply beyond 2030.)

The consequence spreads through every AI capex forecast. A growing portion of infrastructure spending now goes toward higher memory prices rather than proportionally more usable compute.

The third repricing in the industry came through the macro data.

3.3. The Fed Discovered It Was Underwriting the Buildout

On June 25, the Bureau of Economic Analysis raised first-quarter real GDP growth to 2.1% annualized. Investment, information services, professional and technical services, and durable-goods manufacturing were among the major contributors. Meanwhile, real final sales to private domestic purchasers grew only 1.7%.

The BEA does not publish an official “AI contribution to GDP.” One private decomposition estimated that AI-related spending accounted for approximately 76% of first-quarter headline growth. That number depends heavily on which investment categories are labeled AI, so it should be treated as an estimate, not a national-accounting fact.

This means that AI infrastructure has become one of the main engines of American business investment at the same moment that underlying domestic demand was weakening.

Then on June 17, the Federal Reserve held its policy rate at 3.50% to 3.75%. But its projections turned sharply more hawkish. The median projected year-end 2026 rate rose to 3.8%, while the median 2026 PCE inflation forecast rose to 3.6% and core PCE to 3.3%.

Why are we talking about this? Simply put, this creates another kind of loop— AI capex supports economic growth. Stronger growth gives the Fed less room to cut. Higher rates then raise the financing cost of the infrastructure producing that growth.
The companies most exposed are not the cash-rich hyperscalers. They are Oracle, CoreWeave, and the rest of the leveraged layer being paid to build capacity for them.

The Nasdaq demonstrated the sensitivity on June 5. After the US economy added 172,000 jobs, more than twice the consensus estimate, expectations of another rate increase surged and the Nasdaq fell approximately 4.2%.

July update: The June inflation report, released on July 14, weakened the most aggressively hawkish version of this argument. Headline CPI fell 0.4% during June and rose 3.5% year over year, while core CPI was flat for the month and rose 2.6% over the year. That reduced the immediate probability of another increase, but it did not remove the underlying sensitivity of debt-funded infrastructure to interest rates.

There is also one necessary correction before the bears become drunk on their own seriousness.

The viral claim that half of planned 2026 US data-center capacity had been canceled was wrong. found that its forecast for genuine hyperscaler self-build capacity had changed by only around 1% over six months, while its colocation forecast moved by less than 5%.

The apparent collapse came from counting speculative announcements as real projects. Many lacked financing, tenants, equipment orders, interconnection agreements, or construction. In other words, they weren’t really serious projects to begin with.

Demand remained enormous during the same month the credit warnings arrived. Broadcom forecast $16B of AI-chip revenue for fiscal Q3, more than triple the year-earlier level, although the forecast still fell slightly below Wall Street’s expectations. OpenAI had also committed to purchasing up to 750MW of Cerebras capacity, with the expanded agreement reportedly valued above $20B.

Whether this buildout produces enough revenue to justify the capital depends on whether customers continue paying for the agents consuming all this compute. June gave us plenty to examine there.

Section 4. How CFOs Started Metering AI Agents

We’ve been talking about the breakdown of flat-rate agent pricing for a while now. June showed the two replacements: charge customers for the compute agents consume, or charge for outcomes and make the vendor absorb the cost of failure.

4.1. Input Metering Versus Outcome Pricing

On June 1, GitHub replaced Copilot’s premium-request system with GitHub AI Credits, priced at $0.01 per credit and calculated from the input, output, and cached tokens consumed at each model’s API rate. GitHub’s explanation was blunt: under the old system, a short chat request and a multi-hour autonomous coding session could cost the user the same amount, leaving GitHub to absorb the difference, which agentic usage had made “no longer sustainable.”

The base subscriptions remained at $10 per month for Copilot Pro, $19 per user for Business, and $39 for Enterprise, but usage above the included allowance became the customer’s problem, including failed searches, repeated test runs, bad tool calls, and retries. Some users reported projected bills up to 100 times higher, although these were individual reports rather than audited platform-wide data. GitHub added per-user credit consumption to its usage metrics API on June 19 and, by July 2, introduced cost-center credit pools and separate spending caps.

OpenAI moved in the same direction, then partially reversed course. It introduced pay-as-you-go Codex seats for Business and Enterprise customers in April, then stopped offering new pay-as-you-go seats to ChatGPT Business customers on June 24. Existing seats remained active and normal Business subscriptions still included baseline Codex access, but the self-serve path from experimentation to metered deployment lasted less than three months.

Finally, Salesforce took the opposite side of the risk. Its Agentforce Help Agent, launched on June 25 with pay-per-resolution billing scheduled for July, charges only when an issue is resolved autonomously from beginning to end, with no charge when the customer gives negative feedback or requests a human. Salesforce did not publish the per-resolution price in June, but the structure matters more than the number: under input pricing, the customer pays while the agent tries; under outcome pricing, the vendor absorbs the inference cost and gets paid only when it can defend the result as successful.

Salesforce says its own Help site has processed 4.3 million inquiries and autonomously resolved 70% of them, but outcome pricing does not remove verification; it relocates it. Every disputed resolution becomes a billing argument over whether the issue was genuinely closed, whether the user returned, whether a human corrected the answer, and who defines success. Watch every dispute require a 46-page submission of proof, so that most unhappy customers are annoyed into not submitting claims. Such is the nature of progress after all.

Both approaches exist because agents broke the core SaaS assumption that consumption per seat is bounded by human attention. Ramp’s June transaction data showed the resulting spread: across more than 70,000 US businesses, median AI spending was only $11.38 per employee per month, while the top 10% spent roughly $611 and the top 1% spent $7,449. Flat-rate pricing works on the median customer and collapses around the most aggressive users (and as more expensive models become common + more people start using agents, the cost percentiles will shift a lot).

4.2. The Month Demand Learned to Say No

The stronger signal came from buyers:

  • Uber reportedly exhausted its full-year Claude Code budget during the first four months of 2026 and responded by imposing a $1,500 monthly limit per employee for each agentic coding tool, including Claude Code and Cursor.

  • Microsoft offered the more revealing case. Its Experiences and Devices division, which includes Windows, Microsoft 365, Teams, Outlook, and Surface, began canceling most internal Claude Code licences and instructed thousands of employees to move to GitHub Copilot CLI by June 30. Claude models remained available through Copilot; Microsoft removed the separate Claude Code product and consolidated usage onto infrastructure, procurement, and telemetry it controlled.

The decision shows why enterprise buying isn’t as output-dependent as originally thought. A product can improve output and still lose the contract if the buyer owns a close substitute, can integrate it more deeply, and prefers to keep the data, spend, and bargaining power inside its own stack. A Microsoft study released in July found that engineers adopting command-line coding agents merged roughly 24% more pull requests over four months, although the authors warned that merged pull requests are only a proxy for value. Microsoft had internal evidence of measurable productivity gains and still removed most Claude Code licences because it did not need to rent Anthropic’s full product to capture them.

Once costs become visible, enterprises optimize across performance, integration, security, procurement, observability, switching costs, and vendor leverage rather than simply buying whichever model tops a leaderboard. The model can win the benchmark while the vendor loses the budget.

The spending data shows the same pattern. A January survey of 100 Global 2000 executives found that average enterprise LLM spending had risen from about $4.5M to $7M over two years, with respondents expecting another 65% increase during 2026, while 81% were testing or using at least three model families and 65% still preferred incumbent products when available. Budgets were growing, but they were also becoming fragmented, governed, and negotiable. Falling token prices did not produce infinite demand because cheaper intelligence still had to pass through procurement.

4.3. The Tokenizer Became a Pricing Weapon

Once buyers began auditing the bill, the billing unit itself became unstable. Anthropic launched Claude Sonnet 5 on June 30 at an introductory $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, rising to $3 and $15 afterward. Sonnet 5 also introduced a new tokenizer that, by Anthropic’s own estimate, produces approximately 30% more tokens than Sonnet 4.6 for the same input, depending on the content.

Simon Willison’s independent testing found that identical documents produced 1.42 times as many tokens for English, 1.33 times for Spanish, 1.27 times for Python, and almost no change for Simplified Mandarin. On the English sample, the promotional rate therefore translates to an effective $2.84 per million old-token-equivalent input tokens and $14.20 for output, roughly matching Sonnet 4.6’s $3 and $15 pricing. Once the promotion ends, the same text costs an effective $4.26 and $21.30, a 42% increase despite an apparently unchanged price card.

Anthropic disclosed the tokenizer change, so the problem is not concealment. The problem is that vendors trained customers to treat tokens as a common unit while retaining the power to redefine the unit. Dollars per million tokens is not comparable across vendors if one tokenizer turns the same document into 42% more billable units.

The comparison breaks further when the product label no longer identifies one fixed model. Anthropic’s Fable 5 can route certain risky requests to Opus 4.8, while Sakana’s Fugu-Ultra orchestrates Claude Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3.1 Pro through workflows of up to five steps. Fugu reached 73.7 on SWE-Bench Pro against 69.2 for Opus 4.8, not because Sakana trained a single superior base model, but because its router could buy work from several frontier models and combine the results.

That is a legitimate result, but it makes the current comparison framework useless. Benchmarks assume one model answered, price cards assume tokens are comparable, and leaderboards assume test-time compute is bounded or disclosed. Agentic systems can violate all three assumptions at once through routing, retries, hidden reasoning, tool use, and variable compute.

Thus, the only useful comparison is cost per successfully completed task, normalized for the tokenizer and accompanied by the full execution record: models called, retries, cached and uncached context, tools used, human intervention, and failure rate. Even that is difficult because vendors control most of the evidence. A May paper on token billing found that tokenizer ambiguity permitted 50.85% over-reporting below the detection threshold in simulated attacks, while hidden-reasoning inflation reached 1,469% in the most permissive setting.

This means that while a developer could detect Sonnet 5’s tokenizer change with a script, an enterprise audit layer must repeat that exercise continuously across tokenizers, routers, hidden reasoning, tool calls, cache rules, discounts, retries, and outcome definitions. The standards-and-audit market is therefore an attempt to build a trusted meter for a product whose manufacturer controls the ruler.

Credit desks were pricing the buildout, and CFOs were pricing the agents. Buyers then moved more volume toward models that were cheaper, easier to inspect, and harder to switch off.

Section 5. Why Open-Weight Models Took the Token Volume

On June 12, the Commerce Department forced Anthropic to disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 because it could not verify users’ nationalities in real time. Both models disappeared globally on June 12. Mythos 5 partially returned for vetted US critical-infrastructure operators on June 26, Commerce revoked the directive on June 30, and Fable 5 returned globally on July 1. Four days after the shutdown, Z.ai released GLM-5.2 under an MIT licence, with downloadable weights and no regional lock.

That contrast explains most of the open-weight strategy. A hosted model remains controlled by its developer, cloud provider, and home government. Access can be restricted, prices can change, usage can be monitored, and the service can disappear without the customer’s consent. Once open weights have been downloaded and deployed on private infrastructure, no vendor can remotely disable them or rewrite the terms of access. For buyers outside the United States, particularly governments and regulated companies, that is not an ideological preference for open source. It is supply-chain control.

Open weights are also easier to verify. Customers can test the exact model version they will deploy, inspect its outputs across their own workloads, measure its real cost, control the inference environment, and reproduce results without depending on a vendor’s changing API. This does not make the system fully transparent; the training data and complete training process may still remain closed. But it removes several of the largest sources of uncertainty at deployment: hidden model changes, undisclosed routing, shifting tokenizers, regional restrictions, and vendor-controlled usage logs. In a market increasingly organized around auditability, the ability to hold the weights is itself a verification advantage.

The economics then make the decision easier. OpenRouter found that Chinese models had overtaken US models in token volume by early June. Vercel’s June production data showed open-weight models handling 29% of tokens while accounting for less than 4% of spending. DeepSeek alone reached 22.6% of token volume, while proprietary models continued to dominate spending and the most sensitive workloads. The market was not replacing the frontier; it was dividing the work. Proprietary US models retained the tasks where the final increment of capability justified the premium, while cheaper open-weight models absorbed the repetitive, high-volume layer underneath. FYI— we called out how China was explicitly investing in their models to handle this “good intelligence, at low prices” segment (90% of the token volume) all the way back in Feb, as you can see below—

GLM-5.2 showed how quickly that layer could improve. Z.ai kept the same 744B-parameter mixture-of-experts architecture, with 40B active parameters per token, but generated a large performance gain through reinforcement learning, long-context training, distillation, and inference optimization rather than another increase in model size. This matters because the frontier premium now decays faster: once a capability becomes reproducible through post-training, an open model can offer most of it at a fraction of the price and without the vendor dependency. More on how GLM 5.2 accomplishes frontier performance at fractional costs here.

That is the strategic problem for American labs and export-control policymakers. Closed models are easier to regulate, but every restriction makes open weights more valuable to buyers who care about continuity, sovereignty, and verification. Washington can control access to an American API. It cannot recall a model already running on private infrastructure across Europe, India, or the Gulf.

The American frontier remains more capable at the top end, but it is closed, expensive, and permissioned. Chinese open-weight models are increasingly setting the price for everything below that frontier. In a market moving toward verification, the model a customer can inspect, host, and keep may be worth more than the model that scores slightly higher but can be switched off from somewhere else.

What Happens Next

Most enterprise decisions are exercises in avoiding blame.

For a while, benchmarks gave buyers cover. You chose the model at the top of the leaderboard, and if it failed, at least you had followed the numbers. That excuse is becoming harder to use. Benchmarks can be trained against, token prices are not comparable, routers hide how much compute was used, and the best model can still be the wrong commercial product.

Something else will replace them.

That is why verification matters. Audits, approved evals, cost-per-task measurements, trusted-partner status, public margins, and deployment records will become the new evidence buyers hide behind. None of these measures will be perfect. They do not need to be. They only need to make the decision defensible.

This gives power to whoever controls that evidence. Governments decide which models are cleared. Clouds decide which models are bundled, routed, and discounted. Enterprise platforms control usage data and procurement. Auditors decide what counts as safe or effective. The model lab may build the intelligence, but someone else increasingly decides whether it can be bought, how its value is measured, and where it gets deployed.

The labs wanted intelligence to become the platform underneath every industry. The disrupters that would bring the hallowed silicon valley efficiency and pioneering spriti to every segment of the world. They may instead become suppliers inside platforms controlled by governments, clouds, and enterprise software companies.

Life really has a funny sense of humor, doesn’t it?
